figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) shows the typical FR-IQA architecture. Most deep FRIQA models compute the differences between feature maps to predict quality scores. (b) shows the proposed FR-IQA architecture, where we extract content-influenced degradation features to predict quality scores.
Fig. 5. (a) represents the structure of the autoencoder, and (b) is the structure of the autoencoder after intervention.
TABLE I Results of the performance comparison conducted on benchmark datasets.
TABLE II Results of the performance comparison conducted on non-standard natural IQA datasets.
Fig. 13. Example results of the proposed method. For instance, given a distorted image and a reference image, the proposed method attempts to transfer the degradations to the reference image.
TABLE VIII UMAP Parameter Sensitivity and Stability Experiment Results (TID2013 Dataset, 10 Repeats)
TABLE III SROCC and PLCC of different data scales across four datasets.
Fig. 7. Local neighborhood relations in the feature space mainly reflect the proximity of the latent perceptual quality values.

Fig. 3. Illustrations of different distortion visibility. Under the same distortion intensity level, the image with smooth content can be more tolerant to lens blur than the image with textured content, yet more sensitive to quantization noise. The value in parentheses indicates the imposed distortion intensity level.
Fig. 11. Image examples from diverse domains. (a) to (f) are respectively natural image, infrared image, neutron radiography image, screen content image, ultrasonic image, and tone-mapped image..
Fig. 10. The structure of the decoder and causal layer.
